output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.6, "R1.5": 0.3, "R1.6": 0.5, "R1.7": 0.4}}

Summary: Targeted critique of the section's framing and argument chain, with concrete rewrites for the two strongest overclaims and the weakest piece of reasoning.

**Issue 1 — The "independence from deployment distribution" argument is not valid as stated.**
The paragraph claims T_phi transfers to arbitrary test distributions "because" it is a function of internal representations rather than input statistics. This is a non sequitur: internal representations are themselves deterministic functions of the input, so their distribution shifts under distribution shift (this is the whole premise of feature-based OOD detection). What you actually have is empirical evidence that the *mapping from entropy vector to optimal temperature* appears stable across the three shift regimes you tested — not a structural guarantee. Recommend rewriting as:

> "Because T_phi conditions on per-input internal signals rather than on a fixed global temperature, it can in principle adapt to inputs drawn from distributions unseen at calibration time. We show empirically in Sections 4–5 that this adaptation transfers across corruption, sketch, and zero-shot benchmarks; we do not claim a formal guarantee of distribution-free calibration."

**Issue 2 — "Arbitrary deployment distributions" and "broadly transferable" overclaim what 3 + 12 benchmarks can show.**
ImageNet-C, ImageNet-Sketch, and 12 zero-shot datasets are all natural-image distributions reachable from ImageNet pretraining. Soften the abstract-style claim in paragraphs 1 and 4 to something like "a range of covariate-shifted natural-image distributions," and reserve "arbitrary" for the conditions under which you can actually prove or stress-test it (e.g., adversarial shift, medical/satellite domains, label shift — none of which appear here).

**Issue 3 — The baseline is too weak to support a 35–45% headline number.**
Vanilla temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017) is the floor of the post-hoc calibration literature. Reviewers will ask for ETS (Zhang et al., 2020), Dirichlet calibration (Kull et al., 2019), Spline calibration (Gupta et al., 2021), and any recent feature/representation-based calibrators. Either add these baselines or qualify: "relative to vanilla temperature scaling — the standard baseline; comparisons against ETS and Dirichlet calibration appear in Table X."

**Issue 4 — Training T_phi on "a labeled subset of ImageNet-val" raises a leakage flag.**
You then evaluate on "the standard ImageNet-val set." State explicitly that calibration training uses a held-out split disjoint from the ECE evaluation set, and report the split sizes. Otherwise paragraph 3's first result is uninterpretable.

**Issue 5 — The "core insight" is asserted, not motivated.**
"CLS attention entropy across blocks correlates strongly with prediction correctness" is the load-bearing empirical claim of the method. Either (a) forward-reference a figure ("see Figure 2, where per-block CLS entropy yields AUROC X for correctness prediction") or (b) cite prior work that established this relationship. As written, the reader has to take it on faith. Also worth checking whether this overlaps with attention-rollout / attention-based uncertainty work (Abnar & Zuiderveer, 2020; and the line on transformer-feature-based OOD), which should appear in related work to establish delta.

**Issue 6 — "Well-calibrated" vs. "better-calibrated."**
The opening sentence promises "well-calibrated confidence scores" in absolute terms, but the evidence in paragraph 3 is *relative* (a 35–45% ECE reduction). A 40% reduction from a poorly calibrated start can still be poorly calibrated. Either report absolute ECE numbers in the intro ("ECE of 1.2% on ImageNet-val, 3.4% under ImageNet-C severity 5") or rephrase to "substantially better-calibrated."

**Issue 7 — Forward reference to Section 5 reads as a results dump in the intro.**
The last paragraph promises a specific empirical result. Consider trimming to: "Section 5 extends the framework to CLIP-style dual-encoder architectures and tests transfer to zero-shot classification." Let Section 5 present its own number.

**Minor prose:**
- "produces well-calibrated confidence scores under arbitrary deployment distributions" → tighten and de-hype (see Issue 2).
- "The core insight is that…" — fine, but pair with citation/figure.
- "no further tuning on the corruption or sketch distributions" is buried; this is actually one of your strongest selling points and belongs in the first paragraph.
- T_phi vs. $T_\phi$: pick one and use consistently with the rest of the paper.

Uncertainty: I have not verified the existence or current state of the specific calibration baselines I named (ETS, Dirichlet, Spline) at the citation level, nor whether prior work has already proposed CLS-attention-entropy as a calibration signal — both should be checked against your related-work section before adopting the suggested phrasings.
